affordances.name: Objective, constraint, and trade-off fit
affordances.mechanism: Before recommending an allocation, require the true objective, proxy or decision variable, real constraints, and conflict metric to line up; then compare options by how capital, time, risk, headcount, bottlenecks, and interactions change the whole-system result rather than a local indicator.

use_when: The team can say what it is actually maximizing or minimizing and must distinguish the true target from a convenient proxy.
Capital, time, risk limits, headcount, governance boundaries, or operational bottlenecks materially change which option is best.
A recommendation must state what is being sacrificed, what is being protected, and which metric wins when goals conflict.
A measurable indicator, local process metric, or elegant model output could improve while drifting away from the real whole-system outcome.
do_not_use_when: The objective cannot yet be named clearly enough to distinguish the true target from a convenient proxy.
The constraints are decorative and do not materially change which options are feasible or best.
The situation is dominated by unknown unknowns, novelty, or discontinuous system behavior where strict maximization would create false certainty.
The better move is a good-enough threshold because maximizing one objective would ignore diminishing returns.
case_evidence_needed: The true objective function and the real outcome it is supposed to serve.
The measurable proxy, local process metric, decision variable, or model output being improved and why it still tracks the real outcome.
The capital, time, risk, headcount, governance, or bottleneck constraints that materially change the option ranking.
The explicit trade-off: what is sacrificed, what is protected, and which metric wins when goals conflict.
The interaction evidence showing that a local gain does not damage the whole-system result.

treatment_requirements.description: Anchor the recommendation to the real outcome before comparing options, and test whether the metric or variable being improved is still a faithful proxy.
evidence_required: The stated objective or outcome the work is meant to improve.
The measurable proxy, local metric, or model output being used to judge progress.
Evidence that the proxy still tracks the real outcome rather than only the dashboard or local process.
The rejection or revision condition if the proxy has become the target.
good_output_shape: States the true target separately from the convenient proxy.
Explains why the proxy or variable still represents the real outcome.
Rejects metric improvement when the proxy no longer tracks the target.
Avoids best-result language until the objective and proxy are aligned.

treatment_requirements.description: Make constraints operational by showing how actual limits change feasibility, ranking, and trade-off selection across options.
evidence_required: The relevant capital, time, risk, headcount, governance, or bottleneck constraints.
How each constraint changes which option is feasible or best.
The protected outcome, sacrificed outcome, and winning metric under conflict.
The option or scope change required when constraints bind.
good_output_shape: Names real constraints in operational units instead of listing decorative limits.
Shows how constraints change the option ranking.
States what is sacrificed, what is protected, and which metric wins.
Cuts or revises options that only work when the constraints are ignored.

treatment_requirements.description: Before accepting a local metric gain, trace the interaction path from the local change to the whole-system result and block improvements that only optimize a part.
evidence_required: The local process, team, metric, or model output being improved.
The wider system outcome the local change is supposed to serve.
The interaction, dependency, or trade-off path connecting the local change to the whole-system result.
Evidence that the local gain will not move harm into another part of the system.
good_output_shape: Separates local metric movement from whole-system outcome movement.
Names the dependency or interaction that makes the local gain matter.
Flags proxy gains that damage or fail to move the real outcome.
Treats whole-system fit as a requirement, not a decoration after local success.

diagnostic_questions: What are we actually maximizing or minimizing, and is the measure still tracking the real outcome?
Which capital, time, risk, headcount, governance, or bottleneck constraint materially changes which option is best?
What are we sacrificing, what are we protecting, and which metric wins when goals conflict?
Which local metric could improve while the whole-system result gets worse or stays unchanged?
Are we maximizing past the point where a good-enough threshold would capture the value more efficiently?
misuse_guards: Do not optimize a measurable indicator, local process metric, or elegant model output after it stops tracking the real outcome.
Do not invoke best-result language until the objective, variable, constraints, and trade-offs are explicit.
Do not treat local metric improvement as whole-system improvement without tracing the relevant interactions.
Do not force strict maximization in unknown-unknown, novel, or discontinuous systems where de-risking, resilience, or scenario work is the source-backed move.
Do not keep maximizing one objective when diminishing returns make a good-enough threshold more appropriate.

source_evidence.source_quote: Optimization Theory, as a mental model, focuses on the fundamental objective of maximizing desired outcomes or minimizing undesirable variables within a constrained system. It is the systematic pursuit of the *best possible result*.

Optimization is intrinsically linked to defining clear goals. Success is only achieved when execution serves the "right outcome", and all subsequent actions and decisions must align with that overall purpose.
True optimization occurs within limits.
Most useful when the team can say what it is actually maximizing or minimizing and can distinguish the true target from a convenient proxy.
Works when capital, time, risk limits, headcount, governance boundaries, or operational bottlenecks materially change which option is best.
Best when a recommendation should state what is being sacrificed, what is being protected, and which metric wins when goals conflict.
Danger when teams optimize a measurable indicator, local process metric, or elegant model output that no longer tracks the real outcome they claim to care about.
The conductor ensures that the collective output is greater than the sum of its parts, thereby **optimizing the whole** organization.
The entire discipline of systems thinking is oriented toward optimizing complex interactions, moving from seeing static "snapshots" to recognizing interrelationships and patterns of change.
The existence of **Unknown Unknowns** means optimization must shift to de-risking and resilience, rather than strict maximization.
optimization-theory conflicts with satisficing (satisficing) when maximizing one objective ignores diminishing returns that a good-enough threshold would capture more efficiently.

review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed objective clarity, proxy discipline, constraint reality, explicit trade-off selection, and local-vs-whole-system checking into one affordance because the source presents these as the minimum conditions under which optimization is useful rather than generic efficiency rhetoric.
dropped_material: Did not split objective naming and proxy-risk auditing into separate affordances because the source's strongest operational question is whether the named objective and its proxy still track the real outcome.
Did not split local-vs-global optimization into a standalone affordance because it functions here as the required whole-system check before a local metric gain can count.
Did not promote satisficing, non-linear dynamics, knowns-and-unknowns, systems-thinking, or prospect-theory relations into separate runtime affordances.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm the boundary with trade-offs: this affordance should activate when the objective function, constraints, and winning metric must be specified, while trade-offs owns broader sacrifice and hidden-cost discipline.
Reviewer should confirm the boundary with theory-of-constraints: this record handles constraints as feasibility and option-selection limits, not as a single measured throughput cap.

affordances.name: Leverage-bounded analysis before resource spend
affordances.mechanism: Allocate analysis effort by defining the problem and end product, stating a one-day answer or hypothesis, identifying key drivers, and stopping fact gathering once evidence can prove or disprove the hypothesis, so scarce effort goes to leverage rather than completeness.

use_when: The problem is drowning in detail and the team needs to filter nonessential work toward core leverage points.
A strategic or resource-allocation problem needs key drivers and current activities tied back to the overall strategic goal.
The team is at risk of boiling the ocean by starting complex analysis or model building before defining the needed output.
Completeness or accuracy could become useless because the result would not help people make predictions or daily decisions.
do_not_use_when: The situation requires full-distribution downside sizing, scenario planning, or worst-case stress testing before any bounded-analysis shortcut is acceptable.
The problem is genuinely novel enough that a familiar optimization framework would substitute old pattern recognition for discovery.
The source-backed task is to test every possibility or run a portfolio of experiments rather than prune analysis early.
The team has not named the objective clearly enough for key drivers or enough-facts thresholds to be meaningful.
case_evidence_needed: The decision maker's need, problem definition, and end product the analysis must serve.
The one-day answer, hypothesis, or working solution path that can guide analysis.
The key drivers or core leverage points that would change the recommendation.
The facts needed to prove or disprove the hypothesis and the stop condition for additional analysis.
The work, detail, or attractive analysis path the team will not pursue because it does not move the leverage point.

treatment_requirements.description: Require a precise problem frame, one-day answer, or challengeable hypothesis before complex analysis begins.
evidence_required: The problem definition and decision output the analysis must serve.
The one-day answer or coherent solution path available from current evidence.
The hypothesis that analysis is meant to prove, disprove, or revise.
The first facts that would most efficiently challenge the hypothesis.
good_output_shape: States the decision output before listing analyses.
Offers a provisional one-day answer or hypothesis when evidence permits.
Links each proposed analysis step to proving or disproving the hypothesis.
Rejects analysis tasks that do not change the decision output.

treatment_requirements.description: Concentrate effort on the key drivers and explicitly decide what not to do, so resource use follows leverage instead of volume.
evidence_required: The candidate drivers or factors in the problem.
The minority of drivers likely to yield most of the insight or strategic movement.
The current activities or analysis tasks that do not advance the strategic goal.
The analysis, facts, or tasks that will be deferred or killed.
good_output_shape: Names the key drivers before allocating analysis effort.
States what not to do or what to stop gathering.
Connects current activities to the overall strategic goal.
Keeps fact gathering to the level needed to prove or disprove the hypothesis.

treatment_requirements.description: Stop accuracy and completeness work when it no longer improves predictions, daily decisions, or the recipient's ability to act.
evidence_required: The audience or decision maker who must use the output.
The prediction, daily decision, or action the output is supposed to improve.
The added completeness or accuracy currently being pursued.
Evidence that extra precision changes the action or only increases complexity.
good_output_shape: Defines usefulness in terms of predictions, decisions, or action.
Simplifies or stops analysis when extra accuracy does not change behavior.
Names the cost of continuing to analyze.
Avoids presenting an accurate but unusable answer as optimized.

diagnostic_questions: What is the one-day answer or challengeable hypothesis before we start complex analysis?
Which key drivers would change the recommendation if we understood them better?
What facts are enough to prove or disprove the hypothesis?
What analysis, detail, or activity are we deciding not to do because it does not move the leverage point?
Would the added accuracy help people make predictions or daily decisions, or would it only make the answer look more complete?
misuse_guards: Do not start complex analysis or model building before the problem, end product, and hypothesis are framed.
Do not gather facts past the point where they prove or disprove the hypothesis.
Do not treat completeness, accuracy, or exhaustive analysis as value when the output is not usable for decisions.
Do not use a familiar optimization framework on a genuinely novel problem without testing whether the framework fits.
Do not prune analysis when worst-case downside or distribution shape must be stress-tested first.

source_evidence.source_quote: Optimization necessitates filtering out the nonessential to focus on the core leverage points.
Defining the problem precisely
Deciding what to do and what *not* to do, ensuring efforts are focused where they will yield the greatest return.
Using strong hypotheses to guide analysis, which helps to accelerate the discovery of the optimal path by testing assumptions efficiently.

The principle of finding the **key drivers** saves time and effort by directing thorough analysis only where it will yield the most good, keeping the focus on how current activities advance the overall strategic goal.
Ask: **"What's the one-day answer?"** to force a coherent summary and solution path at any stage.
Rigorously frame the question or hypothesis *before* commencing complex analysis. Gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the hypothesis.
Optimizing for completeness or accuracy can be useless if the resulting idea does not help employees make predictions or daily decisions.
**An accurate but useless idea is still useless**.
Applying a favorite or familiar mental model (e.g., a return on capital tree) to every problem, even novel ones, simply because it's readily available.
Use simulations to highlight the range of possible outcomes.

review_notes.normalization_note: Kept leverage-bounded analysis separate from objective and constraint fit because this affordance changes the analysis budget and sequencing: frame the answer or hypothesis, focus key drivers, and stop nonessential fact gathering before completeness becomes waste.
dropped_material: Did not split Pareto, hypothesis-driven work, communication simplicity, and cognitive-load examples into separate affordances because they all serve the same bounded-effort mechanism in this source.
Did not treat portfolio-of-experiments creative testing as part of this affordance because that source-backed example can require broad testing rather than early pruning.
Did not promote one-day-answer wording into a generic speed heuristic; it is retained only as a control against boiling the ocean.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm the boundary with prioritization and pareto-principle records once those Batch 2 extractions exist; this record should own the optimization-specific analysis-budget contract, not general task ranking or 80/20 distribution claims.

absence_records.reason: The source does describe maximizing desired outcomes, but it only becomes operational when the true target, proxy, constraints, and trade-offs are explicit. A generic optimize-the-objective affordance would erase the source-backed safeguards requested for this extraction.

source_evidence.source_quote: Most useful when the team can say what it is actually maximizing or minimizing and can distinguish the true target from a convenient proxy.
Works when capital, time, risk limits, headcount, governance boundaries, or operational bottlenecks materially change which option is best.
Best when a recommendation should state what is being sacrificed, what is being protected, and which metric wins when goals conflict.
Danger when teams optimize a measurable indicator, local process metric, or elegant model output that no longer tracks the real outcome they claim to care about.

absence_records.reason: The source explicitly limits optimization when unknown unknowns, novelty, diminishing returns, or discontinuous response surfaces make strict maximization misleading. Those cases should route to de-risking, resilience, satisficing, non-linear dynamics, or review rather than this affordance.

source_evidence.source_quote: The existence of **Unknown Unknowns** means optimization must shift to de-risking and resilience, rather than strict maximization.

When confronting a *genuinely novel problem*, the instinct to use previously successful optimization models (an **availability heuristic** or **substitution bias**) can lead to disastrously wrong solutions or prevent the discovery of novel, truly optimal paths.
optimization-theory conflicts with satisficing (satisficing) when maximizing one objective ignores diminishing returns that a good-enough threshold would capture more efficiently.
optimization-theory conflicts with non-linear-dynamics (non-linear dynamics) when optimization assumes smooth response surfaces in systems with discontinuous phase transitions.

absence_records.reason: The source mentions Pareto, cognitive load, first principles, game theory, MECE, simulations, pitching, and experiments as examples or neighboring models. Promoting each into an optimization-theory affordance would duplicate adjacent model records and blur the narrower objective-constraint and bounded-analysis contracts.

source_evidence.source_quote: The Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule):
Cognitive Load Optimization:
First Principles Thinking:
Game theory helps work through choices and competitor responses to choose the optimal strategy, often involving simulations.
MECE structures thinking with "maximum clarity (hence minimum confusion) and maximum completeness".
In creative fields, optimization means creating a portfolio of experiments rather than trying to predict winners.
Most useful when the team can say what it is actually maximizing or minimizing and can distinguish the true target from a convenient proxy.
Works when capital, time, risk limits, headcount, governance boundaries, or operational bottlenecks materially change which option is best.
Best when a recommendation should state what is being sacrificed, what is being protected, and which metric wins when goals conflict.
Danger when teams optimize a measurable indicator, local process metric, or elegant model output that no longer tracks the real outcome they claim to care about.
Optimization necessitates filtering out the nonessential to focus on the core leverage points.
Rigorously frame the question or hypothesis *before* commencing complex analysis. Gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the hypothesis.

review_notes.normalization_note: Read the extraction contract, schema, PR 9 Batch 2 selection note, full Optimization_Theory_rag.md source, Wave 1 activation curation, Wave 2 intervention semantics, and Wave 3 relation semantics. Extracted two affordances: one for true-objective, constraint, trade-off, proxy, and local-vs-whole fit; one for leverage-bounded analysis effort. No third affordance was produced, so the Batch 2 third-affordance differentiation test was not triggered.
dropped_material: Did not extract a generic optimize-the-objective affordance because the source-backed model use requires named objectives, real constraints, explicit trade-offs, and proxy discipline.

Did not extract standalone Pareto, cognitive-load, first-principles, game-theory, MECE, simulation, pitching, or portfolio-experiment affordances; those are examples, allies, or adjacent model surfaces rather than distinct optimization-theory contracts here.

Did not promote relation curation for systems-thinking, constraints, leverage-points, confirmation-bias, risk-vs-uncertainty, satisficing, non-linear dynamics, or prospect-theory into separate runtime affordances.
open_questions: Reviewer should check boundaries with trade-offs, pareto-principle, prioritization, and theory-of-constraints once the remaining Batch 2 resource-allocation records are present.
Reviewer should confirm whether the source's structured tension curation dated 2026-04-03 should remain review context or be promoted later by a relation-aware compilation pass.
